revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} qq (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} qq (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")

revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")

revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. revision.comment: Bot: Replaced ill templates with appended |12=draft and created corresponding redirect pages.
revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|12=draft}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|12=draft}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=draft}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=draft}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩|12=draft}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市|12=draft}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=draft}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城|12=draft}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|12=draft}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政|12=draft}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡|12=draft}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|12=draft}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=draft}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|12=draft}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=draft}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=draft}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=draft}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=draft}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=draft}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=draft}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=draft}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}
<!--Nambu Tōji-->
<!--Nambu Tōji-->

revision.text:
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|12=draft}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|12=draft}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=draft}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=draft}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩|12=draft}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市|12=draft}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=draft}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城|12=draft}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|12=draft}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝|12=draft}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政|12=draft}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡|12=draft}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|12=draft}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=draft}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|12=draft}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=draft}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=draft}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=draft}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=draft}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=draft}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=draft}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=draft}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}
<!--Nambu Tōji-->
<!--Nambu Tōji-->

revision.text:
'''南部杜氏'''（なんぶとうじ）とは、[[岩手県]][[花巻市]][[石鳥谷町|石鳥谷（いしどりや）町]]を拠点とする、[[日本酒]]を造る代表的な[[杜氏集団]]の一つ。[[杜氏]]の流派として捉えたときには'''南部流'''（なんぶりゅう）と称される。[[杜氏組合]]としては、全国最大の規模を誇る[[社団法人南部杜氏協会]]を持つ。 ==名称の由来== 通称「[[南部藩]]」と一括して呼ばれる領内（正式には[[盛岡藩]]・[[八戸藩]]・[[七戸藩]]）から出身した杜氏たちを'''南部杜氏'''と呼び、同領内で伝統的に継承されてきた日本酒の醸造技術を'''南部流'''と称するが、彼らがそう自称したわけではなく、他国の人々から「南部から来た杜氏」と他称された呼び方に由来している。 ==歴史== ===発祥の背景=== [[日本酒の歴史#江戸時代|江戸時代]]以前にも、南部藩領内で酒造りは行なわれていたものの、自家醸造の域を出るものではなかった。そこへ[[慶長]]11年（[[1606年]]）ごろ、南部藩の[[御用商人]]であった村井氏・小野氏が、慶長5年（[[1600年]]）に上方の[[伊丹市|伊丹]]において[[鴻池善右衛門]]によって開発された大量仕込み樽の製法を領内にもたらし、藩のバックアップを受けて[[盛岡城]]下で本格的な[[藩造酒]]の生産を始めた。 この時代、領内で醸造にたずさわった酒造技術者は2種類に大別される。一方は、上方などに留学し、先進的な技術を学んで領内に帰り、村井氏・小野氏ら[[近江商人]]の資本のもとに[[江州蔵]]を開いて[[造り酒屋]]を営み始めた、いわば専従杜氏である。他方は、近江商人から酒造りを委託されて、農業の副業として小規模に酒を造っていた農民杜氏である。
完成品のばらつきをできるだけ無くそうと、蔵元は専従杜氏を農村部に巡回させ、農民杜氏の指導に当たらせ、技術レベルの向上を図った。 ===発祥と展開=== 藩、商人、農民に至るまで一体となった藩造酒のプロジェクトは軌道に乗り、[[延宝]]9年（[[1681年]]）には藩領内の造り酒屋の数は189軒を数え、[[寛政]]10年（[[1798年]]）には江戸表からの注文200[[石 (単位)|石]]を受けるまでになった。各地で藩造酒の生産が試みられても、多くが失敗に終わったことを考えると、これは非常に上手くいった例と言わなくてはならない。 中でも[[稗貫郡]][[石鳥谷町|石鳥谷]]（現・花巻市）には藩の[[御用酒屋]]があり、藩主に献上する[[御膳酒]]を造る杜氏である'''酒司'''（さかじこ）が住んでいた。御膳酒はここから警護の兵に守られて盛岡へ運ばれていったという。 こうした藩造酒の技術は農村部で師弟へ伝承されて、やがて藩領の外へ[[出稼ぎ]]に行く杜氏たちを生み出すことになった。南部藩では、表向きは[[明治]]3年（[[1870年]]）まで藩外への出稼ぎを禁止していたが、何かと他の口実を与えてやったり、お目こぼしをしたりして、貧農たちが杜氏として藩外で副収入を得ることを積極的に抑えつけなかった。このことも、南部杜氏が今日では国内最大規模の流派を形成するのに貢献したといえる。 隣接する[[仙台藩]]には多くの杜氏が訪れており、正月頃に[[大崎八幡宮]]へ裸で参詣する行事（現在の[[仙台裸参り]]）を行っていた。 ===明治時代=== 盛岡藩最後の酒司（さかじこ）であった[[稲村徳助]]は、石鳥谷において伝統的な南部流を結集し、明治時代に即した技術として完成させ、多くの弟子を育成したため「近代南部杜氏の祖」とあがめられている。酒造りの神を祀る[[京都市]]の[[松尾神社]]には、60余名の南部杜氏たちが浄財を献じて建てた稲村徳助の顕彰碑がある。 南部杜氏たちの最初に近代的な組織ができたのは、明治36年（[[1903年]]）に設立認可された'''[[岩手県酒造組合]]'''である。このころ、[[日本酒の歴史#明治時代|明治政府]]は[[日清戦争|日清]]・[[日露戦争]]などのために国家予算が増大し、当時[[歳入]]の3割超を占めていた[[酒税]]の増徴によって切り抜けようと、[[日本酒の歴史#醸造業の近代化|醸造業の近代化]]を急いでいた。そして、酒造免許税を[[営業税]]とし、[[造石税]]は1石あたり4円を7円に上げたために、酒造家の抵抗が激しくなった。そのため、政府による酒造家の監督と支配の強化が必要になり、そのための機関として岩手県酒造組合を作らせたのであった。組合長には当時の岩手県知事である[[北条元利]]が就任した。 明治37年（[[1904年]]）には[[国立醸造試験場]]が開設され、当時においては大問題であった[[安全醸造]]の研究が本格化した。明治38年（[[1905年]]）には[[酒造組合法]]が制定され、日露戦争の軍事費の調達のためにさらなる増税が酒税にかけられた。 こうした官製組合の時代を経て、第1回[[全国新酒鑑評会]]が開かれた明治44年（[[1911年]]）にようやく南部杜氏自らの手によって酒造従業員（[[蔵人]]）の組合結成を目的とする'''[[南部醸造研究組合]]'''が創設され、杜氏講習会などが開催されたが、当時は[[蔵元]]と[[蔵人]]は激しく対立する[[資本家]]と[[無産階級]]の関係にあったため、酒造従業員の団結を恐れる酒造家によって、この組合はつぶされていった。 ===大正時代=== [[大正デモクラシー]]の時代となると、ふたたび南部杜氏自らが中心となって、純粋に醸造技術の研鑽と後輩の指導育成、酒造従業員の団結を目的とし、[[大正]]3年（[[1913年]]）に'''[[南部杜氏組合]]'''が結成された。結成当時の組合員数は220名、大正9年（[[1919年]]）には500名、大正12年（[[1922年]]）には1000名と急速に拡大していった。しかし大正12年（1922年）時点での実態的な酒造従業員数は3700名余りと推計されるので、これでも組織率は28%に過ぎない。 大正14年（[[1924年]]）には組合創立十周年記念事業として機関誌「[[トロリ会報]]」が誕生したが、酒造家による弾圧のため第5号をもって発行停止を余儀なくされる。 ===昭和時代以降=== [[昭和]]23年（[[1948年]]）に南部杜氏組合は'''南部杜氏協会'''となり、昭和28年（[[1953年]]）には'''[[社団法人南部杜氏協会]]'''となる。また昭和34年（[[1959年]]）には再び機関誌を発行するようになった。 同協会の会員数は昭和40年（[[1965年]]）の4,153名をピークに、以後減少の一途をたどっている。 ==現況== [[File:Nanbu Toji Kaikan 1.jpg|thumb|南部杜氏会館]] [[平成]]6年（[[1994年]]）の時点で、南部杜氏協会員数は1,725人、杜氏総数は368人、杜氏が就労している蔵数は389場で、[[九州]]と[[沖縄県|沖縄]]以外のほぼ全国に分布している。 また協会内の酒造講習会、自醸清酒鑑評会が頻繁に開催されている。 石鳥谷町（現・花巻市）と[[社団法人南部杜氏協会]]が連携して、石鳥谷を名実ともに「'''南部杜氏の里'''」にしようという事業が進行中である。花巻市には[[南部杜氏伝承館]]、[[南部杜氏会館]]、[[南部杜氏歴史民俗資料館]]、[[石鳥谷農業伝承館]]などの観光施設のほか、後継者育成のための[[南部杜氏研修場]]がある。 ==南部流に属する主な杜氏== * [[辻村勝俊]]（[[青森県]]　[[西田酒造]]　『[[田酒]]』他） * [[佐藤企]]（青森県　[[鳩正宗]]　『[[八甲田おろし]]』他） * [[藤尾正彦]]（岩手県　[[あさ開]]　『[[あさ開]]』） * [[高橋勝男]]（岩手県　[[川村酒造]]　『[[南部関]]』『[[西与右衛門]]』他） * [[菅原万作]]（[[宮城県]]　[[一ノ蔵]]　『一ノ蔵』） * [[高橋藤一]]（岩手県　[[喜久盛酒造]]　『鬼剣舞』『タクシードライバー』） * [[鷹木祐助]]（[[埼玉県]]　[[矢尾酒造]]　『[[矢尾本店|秩父錦]]』） * [[高橋雅幸]]（[[東京都]]　[[田村酒造場]]　『[[嘉泉]]』『[[田むら]]』） * [[多田信夫]]（[[静岡県]]　[[磯自慢酒造]]　『[[磯自慢]]』） * [[関口正俊]]（[[兵庫県]]　[[神結酒造]]　『[[神結]]』他） * [[上戸政萬]]（[[群馬県]]　[[近藤酒造]]　『[[赤城山]]』） * [[中居清]] （[[石川県]]）[[小堀酒造]] 『[[満歳楽]]』） * 関谷海志（宮城県　大和蔵酒造　『雪の松島』） ==関連項目== *[[日本酒]] *[[日本酒の歴史]] *[[杜氏]] *[[小野組#小野家]] ==外部リンク== * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=南部杜氏WEB |date=20010507094342}} * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html 道の駅「石鳥谷」南部杜氏の里] * [http://www.city.hanamaki.iwate.jp/sightseeing/kanko/1205814598352.html 南部杜氏伝承館] {{DEFAULTSORT:なんふとうし}} [[Category:日本酒]] [[Category:青森県の酒]] [[Category:岩手県の酒]] [[Category:日本酒の流派]]

[[関口正俊]]（[[兵庫県]]　[[神結酒造]]　『[[神結]]』他） * [[上戸政萬]]（[[群馬県]]　[[近藤酒造]]　『[[赤城山]]』） * [[中居清]] （[[石川県]]）[[小堀酒造]] 『[[満歳楽]]』） * 関谷海志（宮城県　大和蔵酒造　『雪の松島』） ==関連項目== *[[日本酒]] *[[日本酒の歴史]] *[[杜氏]] *[[小野組#小野家]] ==外部リンク== [http://www.city.hanamaki.iwate.jp/sightseeing/kanko/1205814598352.html 南部杜氏伝承館] {{DEFAULTSORT:なんふとうし}} [[Category:日本酒]] [[Category:青森県の酒]] [[Category:岩手県の酒]] [[Category:日本酒の流派]]

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyogo|ja|伊丹市}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

[[sn:Nambu Tōji]] [[en:Nambu Tōji]] [[ja:南部杜氏]] [[sn:ambu Tōji]]
<!--Nambu Tōji--><!--Nambu Tōji-->

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|qq=}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|qq=}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanamaki City|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Prefecture|qq=}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morioka Domain|qq=}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachinohe Domain|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichinohe Domain|qq=}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyōgo|ja|伊丹市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itami, Hyōgo|qq=}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|qq=}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morioka Castle|qq=}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|qq=}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enpō|qq=}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei|qq=}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hienuki District|qq=}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|qq=}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")

[[sn:Nambu Tōji]] [[en:Nambu Tōji]] [[ja:南部杜氏]] [[sn:ambu Tōji]]
<!--Nambu Tōji--><!--Nambu Tōji-->

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|qq=}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|ja|石鳥谷町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|qq=}}, {{ill|Hanamaki City|ja|花巻市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanamaki City|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Prefecture|qq=}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morioka Domain|qq=}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|ja|八戸藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachinohe Domain|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|ja|七戸藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichinohe Domain|qq=}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami, Hyōgo|ja|伊丹市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itami, Hyōgo|qq=}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|qq=}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|ja|盛岡城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morioka Castle|qq=}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|qq=}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enpō|qq=}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|ja|寛政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei|qq=}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District|ja|稗貫郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hienuki District|qq=}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|qq=}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|ja|大正|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō era|qq=}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]

=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei]] 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")

'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|qq=}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in [[:en:Ishidoriya, Iwate|Ishidoriya, Hanamaki]], [[:en:Hanamaki, Iwate|Hanamaki City]], [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]]. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}} (formally [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], [[:en:Hachinohe Domain|Hachinohe Domain]], and [[:en:Shichinohe Domain|Shichinohe Domain]]). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in [[:en:Itami|Itami, Hyōgo]] by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|qq=}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in [[:en:Morioka Castle|Morioka Castle]] under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|qq=}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By [[:en:Enpō|Enpō]] 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In [[:en:Hienuki District, Iwate|Hienuki District]]’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|qq=}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei]] 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}} (formally [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], [[:en:Hachinohe Domain|Hachinohe Domain]], and [[:en:Shichinohe Domain|Shichinohe Domain]]). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around [[:en:Keichō|Keichō]] 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in [[:en:Itami|Itami, Hyōgo]] by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|qq=}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in [[:en:Morioka Castle|Morioka Castle]] under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|qq=}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By [[:en:Enpō|Enpō]] 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by [[:en:Kansei|Kansei]] 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In [[:en:Hienuki District, Iwate|Hienuki District]]’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|qq=}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|qq=}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]].
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|qq=}}, established in [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|qq=}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|qq=}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei]] 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|qq=}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|qq=}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|qq=}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|qq=}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|qq=}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * [[Tōji]]
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}
{{translated page|ja|南部杜氏|version=99820995|comment=Imported full ja history}}
