revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11408560]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|1=Tōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523596}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Iwate|en|Ishidoriya, Iwate|WD=Q11587296|lt=Ishidoriya, Hanamaki}}, {{ill|Hanamaki, Iwate|en|Hanamaki, Iwate|WD=Q317827|lt=Hanamaki City}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 盛岡藩|1=Morioka Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908854}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|en|Hachinohe Domain|WD=Q7884927|lt=Hachinohe Domain}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|en|Shichinohe Domain|WD=Q7884520|lt=Shichinohe Domain}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami|en|Itami|WD=Q335444|lt=Itami, Hyōgo}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|en|Morioka Castle|WD=Q251650|lt=Morioka Castle}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|en|Enpō|WD=Q1145735|lt=Enpō}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District, Iwate|en|Hienuki District, Iwate|WD=Q5752980|lt=Hienuki District}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|1=gozenshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489263}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, established in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * {{ill|Tōji|en|Tōji|WD=Q7862664|lt=Tōji}}
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}
{{translated page|ja|南部杜氏|version=99820995|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]]
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|1=Tōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523596}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Iwate|en|Ishidoriya, Iwate|WD=Q11587296|lt=Ishidoriya, Hanamaki}}, {{ill|Hanamaki, Iwate|en|Hanamaki, Iwate|WD=Q317827|lt=Hanamaki City}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 盛岡藩|1=Morioka Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908854}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|en|Hachinohe Domain|WD=Q7884927|lt=Hachinohe Domain}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|en|Shichinohe Domain|WD=Q7884520|lt=Shichinohe Domain}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami|en|Itami|WD=Q335444|lt=Itami, Hyōgo}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|en|Morioka Castle|WD=Q251650|lt=Morioka Castle}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|en|Enpō|WD=Q1145735|lt=Enpō}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District, Iwate|en|Hienuki District, Iwate|WD=Q5752980|lt=Hienuki District}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|1=gozenshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489263}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, established in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * {{ill|Tōji|en|Tōji|WD=Q7862664|lt=Tōji}}
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}
{{translated page|ja|南部杜氏|version=99820995|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.

Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * {{ill|Tōji|en|Tōji|WD=Q7862664|lt=Tōji|1=Tōji}}
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:南部杜氏]]
'''Nanbu Tōji''' (南部杜氏) refers to one of the most prominent groups of {{ill|Tōji|ja|杜氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōji|1=Tōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523596}} (master sake brewers) in Japan, based in {{ill|Ishidoriya, Iwate|en|Ishidoriya, Iwate|WD=Q11587296|lt=Ishidoriya, Hanamaki|1=Ishidoriya, Iwate}}, {{ill|Hanamaki, Iwate|en|Hanamaki, Iwate|WD=Q317827|lt=Hanamaki City|1=Hanamaki, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}. The brewing tradition is known as '''Nanbu-ryū''' (南部流) when referred to as a brewing school. The {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professional organization, is the largest of its kind in Japan.
== Origin of the Name == The term "Nanbu Tōji" refers to brewers originating from the territories collectively called {{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 盛岡藩|1=Morioka Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908854}} (formally {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain|1=Morioka Domain}}, {{ill|Hachinohe Domain|en|Hachinohe Domain|WD=Q7884927|lt=Hachinohe Domain|1=Hachinohe Domain}}, and {{ill|Shichinohe Domain|en|Shichinohe Domain|WD=Q7884520|lt=Shichinohe Domain|1=Shichinohe Domain}}). The brewing techniques passed down in these territories are collectively known as "Nanbu-ryū." This name was not self-assigned but originated as an external designation by others referring to these brewers as "Tōji from Nanbu."
== History == === Early Background === Before the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, sake brewing in the Nanbu Domain was limited to home production. Around {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 11 (1606), the merchants Murai and Ono, who served the domain, introduced the brewing techniques developed in {{ill|Itami|en|Itami|WD=Q335444|lt=Itami, Hyōgo|1=Itami}} by {{ill|Kounoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in Keichō 5 (1600). With the domain's support, large-scale brewing began in {{ill|Morioka Castle|en|Morioka Castle|WD=Q251650|lt=Morioka Castle|1=Morioka Castle}} under the framework of {{ill|Han-zōshu|ja|藩造酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-zōshu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (domain-controlled sake brewing).
During this time, brewers in the domain fell into two categories: professional tōji who trained in advanced techniques in regions like Kansai and returned to operate breweries funded by merchants, and part-time farmer brewers who brewed sake on a smaller scale under the guidance of professional tōji.
=== Development and Expansion === By {{ill|Enpō|en|Enpō|WD=Q1145735|lt=Enpō|1=Enpō}} 9 (1681), the number of breweries in the domain had grown to 189, and by {{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei|1=Kansei}} 10 (1798), orders for 200 koku of sake from Edo were being fulfilled. This represented a significant success compared to similar projects in other domains, which often failed.
In {{ill|Hienuki District, Iwate|en|Hienuki District, Iwate|WD=Q5752980|lt=Hienuki District|1=Hienuki District, Iwate}}’s Ishidoriya (now part of Hanamaki), the domain operated official sake breweries producing {{ill|御膳酒|ja|御膳酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御膳酒|1=gozenshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489263}} (sake for the domain lord). This sake was transported to Morioka under guard. These brewing techniques were passed down and eventually led to tōji seeking seasonal work outside the domain.
The domain officially banned tōji from working outside its borders until {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 3 (1870) but tacitly allowed the practice, enabling impoverished farmers to earn supplementary income as tōji. This policy contributed significantly to the Nanbu Tōji's current prominence.
=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, established in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
== Related Articles == * [[Sake]] * [[History of Sake]] * {{ill|Tōji|en|Tōji|WD=Q7862664|lt=Tōji|1=Tōji}}
== External Links == * [http://www.iwatetabi.jp/spot/detail/03209/277.html Nanbu Tōji no Sato] * {{Wayback|url=http://www5b.biglobe.ne.jp/~izmibasi/nanbu/kyoukai.html |title=Nanbu Tōji WEB |date=20010507094342}}

=== Meiji Period === The last official sake brewer of the Morioka Domain, {{ill|Inamura Tokusuke|ja|稲村徳助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inamura Tokusuke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, modernized Nanbu-ryū techniques and trained numerous disciples, earning him the title "Father of Modern Nanbu Tōji." A monument honoring him stands at {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}.
The first modern organization of Nanbu Tōji was the {{ill|Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja|岩手県酒造組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Sake Brewing Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, established in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 36 (1903). The association was part of government efforts to modernize brewing and increase tax revenues.
=== Taishō Period === During the {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō era|1=Taishō era}}, the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Union|ja|南部杜氏組合|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Union|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was established in Taishō 3 (1913). The union aimed to improve brewing techniques and foster solidarity among tōji. Membership grew rapidly, reaching 1,000 by Taishō 12 (1923), though it still represented only 28% of the total workforce.
=== Shōwa Period Onwards === In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23 (1948), the union was restructured as the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Association|ja|南部杜氏協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Association|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and later became an incorporated association in 1953. The association reached a peak membership of 4,153 in 1965 but has since declined.
== Current Status == As of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 6 (1994), the Nanbu Tōji Association had 1,725 members and 368 tōji active in 389 breweries across Japan, excluding Kyushu and Okinawa. Regular workshops and evaluations of sake brewed by members are conducted.
Hanamaki City and the Nanbu Tōji Association collaborate to promote Ishidoriya as the "Home of Nanbu Tōji." Facilities include the {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja|南部杜氏伝承館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja|南部杜氏研修場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Tōji Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and other sites for cultural preservation and training.
== Notable Nanbu Tōji == * {{ill|Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja|辻村勝俊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsujimura Katsutoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Aomori, Nishida Brewery, "Tazake") * {{ill|Takahashi Katsuo|ja|高橋勝男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahashi Katsuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Iwate, Kawamura Brewery, "Nanbu Seki") * {{ill|Tada Nobuo|ja|多田信夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Nobuo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shizuoka, Isojima Brewery, "Isojima")
